content: Homeostatic Regulation was formally named by Walter B. Cannon (1929), building on Claude Bernard's (1857) concept of the milieu intérieur.
Clark L. Hull (1943) established that physiological deficits generate motivational drive states that direct behaviour toward need-satisfying outcomes.
Conant and Ashby (1970) proved that every good regulator of a system must be a model of that system, providing the cybernetic foundation for homeostatic regulation.
Keramati and Gutkin (2014) formally unified homeostatic regulation with reinforcement learning by redefining primary reward as drive reduction.
Friston (2010) recast homeostasis within the Free Energy Principle, providing a probabilistic predictive-coding account of body regulation.
Postulate homeostatic-regulation:P1 claims that deviations of any physiological dimension from its setpoint generate a drive monotonically related to the probability of corrective action.

Postulate homeostatic-regulation:P2 claims that reward-seeking and physiological stability are mathematically equivalent objectives under temporal discounting when reward is defined as drive reduction.
Postulate homeostatic-regulation:P3 claims that agents resist physiological disorder by minimising variational free energy, which upper-bounds sensory surprise.
Postulate homeostatic-regulation:P4 claims that homeostatic control extends to allostasis, whereby setpoints and actions are adjusted in anticipation of future perturbations.

Postulate homeostatic-regulation:P5 claims that adaptive homeostatic behaviour requires an internal model of the physiological system and associative learning over a joint internal-external state space.
Metabolic costs: move=0.05, stay=0.02; energy gain from eating=0.3
The drive D(H(t)) is a scalar motivational signal that equals zero at the homeostatic setpoint and increases monotonically with physiological deviation.
Primary reward r(H(t), K(t)) in the homeostatic-regulation paradigm equals D(H(t)) minus D(H(t)+K(t)), i.e., the drive reduction produced by an outcome.
The temporal discount factor γ must be strictly less than 1 for homeostatic optimality; γ = 1 breaks the equivalence between reward maximisation and deviation minimisation.
The homeostatic state vector H(t) evolves as H(t+1) = H(t) + K(t), where K(t) is the additive outcome impact vector.
The homeostatic reward prediction error δ(t) is the temporal-difference error computed from drive-reduction reward and is hypothesised to be carried by phasic dopamine modulated by the hypothalamus.
Variational free energy F is formally equivalent to the drive D(H(t)) under re-parameterisation: D(H_t) = −ln p(H_t), linking HRL and the Free Energy Principle.
The interoceptive prediction error ε(t) is propagated upward through the neural hierarchy and drives both model updating (learning) and action selection (body regulation).
The behavioural policy π is a mapping from the joint external-plus-internal state to action probabilities, optimised to maximise discounted drive reduction.
Interoceptive sensory precision σ² (inverse variance) determines the relative weighting of bottom-up sensory data versus top-down priors in inferring internal physiological state.
The Hypothalamus is the primary locus of classical homeostatic control, monitoring and regulating temperature, osmolality, glucose, and energy balance while modulating dopaminergic reward circuits.
The Ventral Tegmental Area generates dopaminergic reward prediction error signals whose amplitude is modulated by hypothalamic interoceptive state signals encoding current drive level.
The Nucleus Accumbens receives dopaminergic RPE signals and updates homeostatic action values; it mediates motivational salience of food-predictive cues under hunger.
The Insular Cortex serves as the central hub for interoceptive inference, integrating visceral afferents with contextual information through hierarchical prediction and prediction-error units.
Hyperpalatability causes over-consumption because orosensory signals systematically overestimate the actual homeostatic impact K(t) of the outcome, inducing intake beyond physiological need.
Distance discretization: 0 (at resource), 1-2 (near), 3-4 (med), 5-7 (far), 8+ (very far)
Energy discretization: 5 equal bins over [0.0, 1.0] range
Implements decide method: read-only state perception and action selection via softmax sampling

Uses closed-form single-step Bayesian filtering with precision-weighted Gaussian conjugate updates.

Eq. 1 computes the next energy level as the current energy level minus metabolic decay cost and movement cost, plus energy gained from eating, clipped to [0, 1].
Eq. 2 computes drive as the n-th power absolute deviation of energy level from the setpoint, capturing convex penalty for deviation.
Eq. 3 defines reward as the decrease in drive from the current time step to the next, operationalizing drive-reduction theory.
Eq. 4 computes the temporal-difference error as the immediate reward plus discounted maximum future Q-value minus the current Q-value estimate.
Setpoint h* (default 0.8) represents the ideal normalized energy target, sourced from Keramati & Gutkin (2014).
Drive exponent n (default 2) controls the convexity of the drive function, sourced from Keramati & Gutkin (2014) for quadratic convexity.
Energy decay rate c_dec (default 0.02 per step) represents the metabolic cost incurred at each time step, calibrated.
Movement energy cost c_move (default 0.005) specifies the extra metabolic cost incurred per move action, calibrated.
Discount factor γ (default 0.95) discounts future rewards in Q-learning, required < 1 for homeostatic optimality per Keramati & Gutkin (2014).

Formulation.homeostatic-reinforcement-learning:drive-reduction-td-q-learning-model-free -[HAS_PARAMETER]-> Parameter.homeostatic-reinforcement-learning:drive-reduction-td-q-learning-model-free:lambda-drift

Parameter c_food / c_eat (energy_gain_from_eating / eating restoration) = 0.3, representing ~30% immediate restoration of energy capacity per meal, calibrating the immediate energy restoration value of consuming food (Jacquier 2016).
Parameter c_move (movement cost = 0.005) calibrates the metabolic cost of locomotor actions.

Run 80d9be2e-8521-4e58-b982-9c1da697297c contradicted memory 2f74d6b7-f6a4-4e18-ab4f-3b0c35e9c413: Parameter c_move (movement cost = 0.01) is a simulation parameter controlling navigation cost. → Parameter c_move (movement cost = 0.005) calibrates the metabolic cost of locomotor actions.
Parameter D_crit (critical drive threshold = 0.15) triggers mode switch to foraging when drive reaches or exceeds this value.
Parameter D_low (low drive threshold = 0.02) triggers rest mode when drive falls below this value.
The decision logic implements urgency gating: REST mode when D(t) < D_low, EAT mode when food is available and D(t) > 0, FORAGE mode when D(t) ≥ D_crit or dD/dt > 0, otherwise REST.
In FORAGE mode, the agent computes resource gradient g(a) for each movement action and selects the action with maximum gradient (closest approach to food), implementing reactive cybernetic control.
The formulation is based on Cannon (1929) and Hull (1943) drive-reduction theory, realizing negative-feedback homeostatic regulation with cybernetic action selection.

Eq. 1 computes energy dynamics: energy decreases by natural decay λ, increases by eating restoration c_eat when eating, decreases by movement cost c_move when moving, and includes stochastic noise w(t).
Eq. 2 defines the likelihood model: interoceptive observation o(t) is a noisy measurement of true energy h(t) with Gaussian noise variance σ²_s.
Eq. 3 performs Bayesian state inference via Kalman update: the agent's believed energy μ(t) is a weighted combination of prior prediction and observation, where weight K(t) is the Kalman gain.
Eq. 3a predicts the prior energy mean by projecting forward under the generative model dynamics.
Eq. 3b computes Kalman gain K(t) as the ratio of prior uncertainty to total uncertainty (prior + sensory noise).
Eq. 4 updates posterior variance: uncertainty decreases when a noisy observation is received, proportional to Kalman gain.
Eq. 5 computes interoceptive prediction error as the deviation between observation and prior prediction, driving belief updates in active inference.
Eq. 6 defines pragmatic value as negative squared deviation from setpoint h*, implementing goal-directed homeostatic control.
Eq. 7 computes epistemic value as expected Bayesian surprise (KL divergence), measuring how much the agent expects to reduce uncertainty by taking action a.
Eq. 8 combines pragmatic and epistemic values into expected free energy G(a), with epistemic weight κ balancing exploitation and information-seeking exploration.
Parameter h* (setpoint) defines the preferred internal state toward which homeostatic control aims; default 0.8 from Friston (2010).

Parameter σ²_s (sensory noise variance, default 0.5) represents interoceptive channel noise and is derived from perceptual noise measurements in empirical cue-combination tasks. Lower values of σ²_s increase reliance on observations in Kalman filtering.
Parameter λ (natural energy decay) models metabolic drain; default 0.02 per step represents baseline energy expenditure.
Parameter c_food (also referred to as c_eat, default 0.3) specifies the normalized energy gain (energy restoration) when eating, calibrating the payoff of food consumption.
Parameter c_move quantifies movement cost; default 0.005 reflects metabolic cost of navigation.

Parameter β_G (action selection inverse temperature) defaults to 4.0 following Friston et al. (2011) precision parametrization; controls stochasticity of policy execution. Higher values of β_G increase deterministic action selection by producing a sharper softmax distribution over policies.
Parameter κ (epistemic weight) balances pragmatic homeostatic goals against epistemic exploration; default 0.5 equally weights both.
The active inference framework unifies perception (Bayesian filtering of internal state) and action (minimization of expected free energy) under a single generative model.
Drive function D(h_t) = m·|h_t - h*|^n computes the deviation from homeostatic setpoint with a superlinear penalty (quadratic by default, n=2).
Internal state dynamics h_{t+1} = clip(h_t + λ_drift - K·ate_t, 0, h_max) models metabolic drift increasing hunger and resource consumption reducing hunger.
Homeostatic reward r_t = D(h_t) - D(h_{t+1}) measures drive reduction as the change in deficit from current to next timestep.
Homeostatic RPE δ_t = r_t + γ·max_a' Q(s_{t+1}, h_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, h_t, a_t) computes temporal-difference error for Q-value updates.

Q-values are updated incrementally per rule R4: Q(s_t, h_t, a_t) ← Q(s_t, h_t, a_t) + α·δ_t, where α is the learning rate and δ_t is the reward prediction error (RPE). This update increments the expected discounted return proportionally to the RPE and learning rate.

Action probability P(a | s_t, h_t) follows a softmax distribution: P(a | s_t, h_t) = exp(β·Q(s_t, h_t, a)) / Σ_j exp(β·Q(s_t, h_t, j)), implementing stochastic choice from Q-values (Equation 7). The inverse temperature β controls the exploration–exploitation trade-off, with higher β yielding more deterministic (greedy) action selection.
Drive exponent n=2 from Keramati & Gutkin (2014) ensures quadratic superlinear penalty for deviations from setpoint.
Drive scaling m=1.0 from Keramati & Gutkin (2014) normalizes the drive computation.
Discount factor γ=0.95 from Keramati & Gutkin (2014) must be < 1 for homeostatic optimality.
Learning rate α = 0.1, from standard TD-learning / Q-learning (Sutton & Barto, 1998). Controls the step size of value updates in temporal-difference methods.
Homeostatic setpoint h*=0.0 represents the ideal internal state with no deficit.
Maximum internal state h_max=10.0 sets the simulation bound on internal state.
Resource nutritive value K=3.0 specifies the per-resource drive reduction when consumed.
Metabolic drift rate λ_drift=0.1 models per-timestep increase in hunger due to metabolic cost.
Internal state discretization h_bins=10 defines the number of bins for tabular representation of internal state space.

Drive D(h) is a power-law function of hunger deviation from setpoint, scaled by exponent n (default 2) and scaling factor m (default 1.0), derived from Keramati & Gutkin (2014) postulates on drive theory.

Immediate drive reduction from eating ΔD_eat is computed as the difference between current drive and drive after consumption plus metabolic drift, with consumption amount K (default 3.0 units) sourced from simulation design.
Proximity gain Prox(a) for movement action a is the decrease in Manhattan distance to the nearest resource resulting from the action.

Drive-weighted proximity utility U_move(a) modulates movement valuation by multiplying proximity gain with current drive level D(h_t) and proximity weight w_prox (default 0.5), implementing alliesthesia (Cabanac 1971).
Eating utility U_eat equals the immediate drive reduction ΔD_eat if a resource is present at current position s_t, otherwise −∞, with satiation gating at drive threshold η (default 0.5).
Staying utility U_stay is always non-positive, representing the expected drive cost from metabolic drift λ_drift (default 0.1 per step) without consuming food.
Action selection follows a softmax policy over utilities with inverse temperature β (default 5.0), where higher β increases determinism.
Drive exponent n (default 2) from Keramati & Gutkin (2014) determines the curvature of the drive-deficit relationship.
Drive scaling parameter m (default 1.0) from Keramati & Gutkin (2014) sets the magnitude of drive response to deviation.
Resource nutritive value K (default 3.0) is fixed at simulation design and determines hunger reduction per consumption event.
Metabolic drift rate λ_drift (default 0.1) is fixed at simulation design and represents hunger increase per time step.
Homeostatic setpoint h* (default 0.0) defines the no-deficit ideal and is held constant.
Maximum hunger h_max (default 10.0) is a simulation bound defining the upper clipping limit for hunger state.
Proximity weight w_prox (default 0.5) from tuning balances the contribution of spatial approach versus immediate reward in movement valuation.
Satiation threshold η (default 0.5) prevents over-consumption by setting eating utility to −∞ when drive D(h_t) falls below this threshold.
The policy is memoryless and reactive, computing one-step lookahead utilities based only on current state h_t and position s_t, with no learned Q-table or long-term planning.
Alliesthesia effect (postulate P4): when drive approaches zero (near setpoint), U_move approaches zero, rendering the agent indifferent to approaching food regardless of proximity.
Eq. 1 defines the generative prior as a Gaussian distribution N(h | h*, σ_p²) centered at homeostatic setpoint h* with precision σ_p, encoding the agent's preference for a specific internal state.
Eq. 2 specifies the interoceptive observation model as a Gaussian likelihood N(o_h | h_t, σ_obs²), modeling noisy perception of true hunger state with sensory noise σ_obs.
Eq. 3a and 3b implement Bayesian belief update via precision-weighted averaging, where the posterior mean μ_h combines prior and observed signals weighted by their respective precisions.
Eq. 4 computes pragmatic (homeostatic) value G_prag(a) as the negative expected drive, measuring how well action a reduces expected deviation from setpoint after one step.
Eq. 5 computes epistemic value G_epist(a) as the sum of binary entropy H[R_beliefs(c)] over reachable cells, measuring exploration value toward reducing uncertainty about resource locations.
Eq. 6 combines pragmatic and epistemic values via linear scalarization: G(a) = G_prag(a) + w_e * G_epist(a), where epistemic weight w_e balances exploitation vs. exploration.
Eq. 7 implements action selection as softmax over expected free energy with inverse temperature β_G, converting free energy differences into probabilities.

Run 80d9be2e-8521-4e58-b982-9c1da697297c contradicted memory 8a279013-60fe-467c-90a0-4bbfd87d5277: Equation 9 (action selection) uses a softmax over negative scaled expected free energies with inverse temperature β_G: lower β_G produces more uniform action exploration, higher β_G produces deterministic greedy selection. → Eq. 7 implements action selection as softmax over expected free energy with inverse temperature β_G, converting free energy differences into probabilities.
Eq. 8 defines internal state transition with metabolic drift λ_drift (increase in hunger) and resource consumption (decrease by K), clipped to [0, h_max] bounds.
Eq. 9 updates resource belief map via observation-driven confidence increase and decay of unvisited cells, implementing belief learning without explicit value function.
The setpoint h* (default 0.0) from Keramati & Gutkin (2014) defines the target internal state that minimizes homeostatic cost.
Prior precision σ_p (default 1.0) from Friston (2010) controls homeostatic regulation strength: tighter prior (smaller σ_p) produces stronger drive to maintain setpoint.
Interoceptive observation noise σ_obs (default 0.5) from Petzschner et al. (2021) quantifies uncertainty in sensing of true internal state.
Resource nutritive value K (default 3.0) is a simulation parameter controlling how much eating reduces hunger, calibrating resource effectiveness.
Metabolic drift λ_drift (default 0.1) is a simulation parameter specifying the rate at which hunger increases per timestep without food intake.
Maximum hunger bound h_max (default 10.0) is a simulation parameter defining the physiological upper limit on internal state.
Epistemic weight w_e (default 0.3) is a simulation parameter balancing pragmatic drive reduction against exploratory drive to reduce resource uncertainty.
Resource belief decay κ (default 0.05) is a simulation parameter controlling the rate at which unvisited cells lose believed resource probability.
The formulation unifies homeostatic drive reduction with epistemic exploration by expressing drive as negative log-prior density, following Keramati & Gutkin (2014) and Friston (2010).
Alliesthesia emerges in Formulation 3 when μ_h ≈ h* (agent at setpoint), causing G_prag(eat) ≈ 0 and eliminating food-seeking behavior despite food availability.
Variational free energy F(t) is the precision-weighted sum of squared sensory prediction error (ε_s) and prior prediction error (ε_p), serving as a proxy for surprise under the free-energy principle.
Sensory prediction error ε_s(t) is the mismatch between the interoceptive observation s(t) and the belief state μ(t).
Prior prediction error ε_p(t) is the mismatch between the belief state μ(t) and the interoceptive prior (setpoint) μ_p.

Belief state μ evolves via gradient descent on variational free energy, pulled toward both the sensory observation (via ε_s) and the prior setpoint (via ε_p), with balance determined by their respective precisions π_s and π_p.
Predicted free energy F_pred(a) estimates the variational free energy that would result from taking action a, considering both sensory and prior prediction errors under anticipated future states.

Softmax action selection uses inverse temperature parameter β for the exploration-exploitation tradeoff. Specifically, actions are selected via a softmax policy over negative predicted free energy, where higher β increases exploitation of low-free-energy actions and lower β encourages broader exploration.
Interoceptive setpoint μ_p (default 1.0) represents the ideal 'fully sated' energy level and is normalized to the interval [0, 1].
Sensory noise standard deviation σ_s (default 0.2) captures moderate interoceptive noise; Petzschner et al. (2021) note that interoceptive signals are inherently noisy.
Eating action predicts energy increase of 0.3 (or up to maximum 1.0), implementing the agent's anticipation that consuming a resource raises internal physiological state.
Moving action predicts energy decrease of 0.05 (or 0.03 if moving toward nearest resource), modeling the metabolic cost of locomotion.
Stay action predicts minimal energy decrease of 0.02, reflecting low metabolic cost of inaction.

Drive D(h(t)) equals the multiplicative-exponential distance from physiological setpoint, implementing homeostatic displacement equivalent to negative log-likelihood under a Gaussian prior centered at h*.
Primary reward r(t) is defined as the reduction in drive achieved by transitioning from h(t) to h(t+1); positive reward moves the agent toward setpoint, negative reward away.
Action selection uses softmax over Q-values with inverse temperature β parameter; higher β reduces exploration and increases exploitation of high-value actions.
Physiological state h(t+1) evolves by applying action-dependent outcome K(a) to current state, clipped to [0,1]: eating adds energy, movement and staying subtract metabolic costs.
Homeostatic setpoint h* is fixed at 0.8 per Keramati & Gutkin (2014), reflecting a sub-maximal physiological optimum.
Drive sensitivity m = 4.0 is a multiplicative constant scaling the distance function; drives are non-zero only when h(t) ≠ h*.
Drive exponent n = 2.0 produces quadratic displacement, approximating Euclidean distance in homeostatic space per Keramati & Gutkin (2014).
Temporal discount factor γ = 0.9 ensures convergence and physiological rationality by discounting future drive reductions.
Action inverse temperature β = 8.0 moderates exploration-exploitation; temporarily increases to 15.0 when h(t) < 0.2 (critical energy) to enforce rapid corrective action.
Metabolic cost of movement c_move = 0.05 reduces h(t) per step taken; metabolic cost of staying c_stay = 0.02 is minimal resting cost.
Energy gain from eating k_eat = 0.3 normalizes resource intake; eat action is only valid if a resource exists at the current position.
State representation discretizes continuous (position, energy) space into 25 bins (5 distance × 5 energy), enabling tabular Q-learning.
Decision logic chains perception → state encoding → Q-value lookup → action sampling → execution → reward computation → Q-value update, implementing a full reinforcement learning loop.

Eq. 1 (prior prediction) computes the expected state at time t as a weighted average of the previous posterior and a default baseline, with temporal discount γ controlling the decay toward baseline in the absence of confirming evidence.
Eq. 2 (prediction error) quantifies the mismatch between the current sensory observation s(t) and the prior prediction μ_0(t), serving as the core signal for belief revision.

Eq. 3 (posterior belief) implements the exact conjugate Gaussian posterior mean as a precision-weighted average of observation and prior prediction; high sensory precision π̂_s causes the posterior to track observations, while high prior precision π̂_0 causes it to be dominated by the prior.
Eq. 4 (posterior precision) shows that posterior precision is simply the sum of sensory and prior precisions, reflecting increased certainty when both signals are reliable.

Eq. 5 (precision-weighted surprise) computes a scalar summary of prediction error weighted by sensory precision and normalized by total posterior precision; high w(t) indicates a genuinely surprising and belief-shifting observation.

Eq. 6 (sensory precision adaptation) increases sensory precision when prediction errors are small (the world is predictable) and decreases it when errors are large, implementing a form of precision learning and uncertainty quantification.

Eq. 7 (cell value for movement) evaluates each neighboring cell by combining believed resource value weighted by sensory precision and a distance penalty weighted by relative reliance on prior beliefs; higher prior uncertainty (lower π̂_0) discourages long moves.

Equation 3/8 (softmax action probability): The policy assigns action probabilities proportional to exponentials of β-scaled Q-values, performing softmax action selection with inverse temperature β controlling the degree of exploration. Specifically, low β makes the policy more exploratory (approaching uniform random), while high β makes it more greedy (concentrating probability on the highest-valued action).
Parameter π̂_{s,0} (initial sensory precision, default 4.0) sets high confidence in sensory observations at initialization, sourced from Friston (2010).
Parameter π̂_{0,0} (initial prior precision, default 1.0) sets moderate confidence in prior predictions at initialization, sourced from Friston (2010).
Parameter α_π (precision learning rate, default 0.15) controls the speed of sensory precision adaptation in response to prediction errors, sourced from empirical tuning and Friston (2010).

Parameter γ (temporal discount on prior, default 0.9) controls the rate at which prior predictions drift toward the default baseline in the absence of new observations, sourced from Friston & Kiebel (2009).

Parameter β (action softmax inverse temperature, default/standard value = 5.0) controls the stochasticity of action selection via softmax, governing the degree of exploration: higher β makes selection more deterministic (less exploratory), while lower β increases stochasticity (more exploratory). 5.0 is the standard value used for softmax policies.
Parameter r_{empty} (penalty for failed eat, default −0.5) defines the negative reinforcement when the agent attempts to eat but no food is present, encouraging accurate predictions.
The formulation implements predictive coding via conjugate Gaussian inference, deriving a single-step algebraic belief update that requires no iterative gradient descent or ODEs.

The decision logic follows a perceive-predict-update-evaluate-select cycle, where the agent perceives observations, computes predictions, updates beliefs in closed form, evaluates actions via precision-weighted value, and selects actions via softmax sampling.

content: The formulation 'Homeostatic Reinforcement Learning (HRL) with Drive-Reduction Reward' belongs to the homeostatic-regulation paradigm.
The formulation is grounded in Keramati & Gutkin (2014), who proved that reward-maximisation and homeostatic stability are mathematically equivalent under temporal discounting.
The agent's energy level h is a scalar normalised to [0,1] and decays each timestep by c_dec=0.02.
Moving incurs an additional energy cost c_move=0.005 per step beyond baseline decay.
Energy gain from eating food (c_food / c_eat = 0.30) is normalized by clipping to a maximum of 1.0 to maintain a bounded energy state.
Drive D is computed as |h - h*|^n, making it zero at the setpoint and monotonically increasing with deviation.
The drive exponent n=2 enforces quadratic (convex) drive, ensuring smooth gradients and accelerating urgency.
The setpoint h_star=0.8 is the ideal energy level; both deficits and excesses generate positive drive.
Reward r is defined as D(t) - D(t+1): the reduction in drive produced by an action; positive when drive decreases.
The internal drive-reduction reward r is used for learning; the environment's ConsumeEffect reward of 1.0 is explicitly NOT used.
The Q-table maps discretised state s=(x, y, energy_bin) to action values and is initialised to 0.0 for all entries.
The discount factor gamma=0.95 is strictly less than 1, which Keramati & Gutkin (2014) require for homeostatic optimality.
Action selection uses softmax over integrated values with inverse temperature beta = 5.0, providing moderate exploration-exploitation balance.
The energy dimension of the state space is discretised into n_energy_bins=10 bins covering [0,1].
The state s is a tuple (x, y, energy_bin) where energy_bin = int(clip(h * n_energy_bins, 0, n_energy_bins - 1)).

Agent position and food source locations are read from perception['x'], perception['y'], and perception['resources']['food']. Food sources should be accessed safely via perception['resources'].get('food', []) to default to an empty list when the key is absent.
Whether food was eaten is mapped from last_action_result.get('consumed', False) when the last action was 'eat'.
B3 can be tested by setting h=0.9, executing eat, and asserting r < 0 because drive increases when energy overshoots setpoint.
Drive velocity dD_dt is approximated by the finite difference D - D_prev, indicating whether the organism's condition is improving or worsening.
The behavioural mode is selected by threshold gating: REST if D < D_low, EAT if food is present at cell and D > 0, FORAGE if D >= D_crit or dD_dt > 0, otherwise REST.
No learning occurs in this formulation; the agent is a purely reactive cybernetic negative-feedback controller.
The setpoint h_star=0.8 is matched to the Keramati & Gutkin (2014) normalised energy target.
The drive exponent n=2 encodes quadratic urgency as justified by Hull (1943) and Keramati & Gutkin (2014).
Movement cost c_move=0.005 is subtracted from energy each time the agent executes a movement action.
Eating restoration c_eat=0.3 is added to energy per successful consumption event.
D_crit=0.15 is the critical drive threshold that triggers urgent FORAGE mode.
D_low=0.02 is the low drive threshold below which the agent enters REST mode to conserve energy.
In FORAGE mode, the agent selects the movement action that maximises the Manhattan-distance gradient toward the nearest food resource.
The external environment reward signal (ConsumeEffect reward=1.0) is ignored; the agent is driven solely by internal drive dynamics.
B1 (immediate foraging when depleted) can be tested: set h=0.4, assert mode == 'FORAGE' and agent moves toward nearest food.
B1 is testable because h=0.4 yields D=(0.8-0.4)^2=0.16 > D_crit=0.15, guaranteeing FORAGE mode selection.
B2 (rest when sated) can be tested: set h=0.85, compute D=max(0.8-0.85,0)^2=0, assert D < D_low and action == 'stay'.
B2 is testable because any h >= h* produces D=0 which is below D_low=0.02, deterministically selecting REST.
B3 (eat immediately when on food and hungry) can be tested: place agent on food cell with h=0.5, assert action == 'eat'.

B3 is testable because h=0.5 gives D=(0.8-0.5)^2=0.09 > 0, and food present at cell triggers EAT mode before FORAGE.
B4 (energy oscillates around setpoint) can be tested: run 500 steps with food regenerating and assert 0.6 < mean_h < 0.9.
B4 requires a simulation run and is testable as a statistical assertion over the time series of h.
B5 (drive velocity detects worsening) can be tested: set h=0.68 (D=0.0144 < D_crit) with D_prev=0.01 so dD_dt=0.0044 > 0, assert mode == 'FORAGE'.
B5 is testable because the dD_dt > 0 branch in R5 fires independently of D >= D_crit, enabling preemptive mode switching.
Perception maps position to (perception['x'], perception['y']) for use in gradient computation.
The agent maintains a generative model of its internal energy state and infers true energy under interoceptive noise via Kalman filtering.
Action selection is performed by minimising expected free energy G, combining pragmatic value (setpoint proximity) and epistemic value (information gain).
The agent performs one-step lookahead over all available actions rather than learning a Q-table or explicit action-value function.
The homeostatic setpoint h_star is set to 0.8, grounded in Friston (2010) Free Energy Principle.

Process noise variance sigma2_w is set to 0.04, sourced from Petzschner et al. (2021), and was originally labelled sigma2_p (prior precision) in the formulation but resolved as process noise variance for the Kalman filter.
Sensory noise variance sigma2_s is set to 0.01, representing interoceptive channel noise per Petzschner et al. (2021).
Natural energy decay lambda_decay is 0.02 per step, calibrated to represent metabolic drain.
Eating restores energy by c_eat = 0.3 per food item consumed.
Movement incurs an extra metabolic cost of c_move = 0.005 per move action.
The softmax inverse temperature beta_G is 4.0, providing moderate sharpness in action selection.
The epistemic weight kappa is 0.5, balancing exploration (information gain) vs. exploitation (pragmatic value).
The true energy level h is hidden and ranges in [0.0, 1.0] with initial value 0.8.
The believed energy level mu is the Kalman-filter posterior mean estimate of h, initialised at 0.8.
The posterior variance sigma2_q represents Kalman-filter uncertainty about the internal state and converges to a steady-state value.
The interoceptive observation o is computed as h plus Gaussian noise with variance sigma2_s (Rule R2).
The prior prediction mu_prior is computed from the last posterior mu adjusted for the previous action's effect on energy dynamics (Rule R3a).
The Kalman gain K_gain = sigma2_q_prior / (sigma2_q_prior + sigma2_s), balancing trust between prior and observation (Rule R3c).
The posterior update follows mu = clip(mu_prior + K_gain * (o - mu_prior), 0.0, 1.0) (Rule R3d).
The interoceptive prediction error epsilon_int = o - mu_prior represents Bayesian surprise (Rule R5).
Pragmatic value V_prag_a = -((mu_predicted_a - h_star)^2) measures negative squared deviation from setpoint for candidate action a (Rule R6).
Epistemic value V_epist_a = 0.5 * ln(sigma2_q_prior_a / sigma2_q_post_a) measures expected entropy reduction from taking action a (Rule R7).
Expected free energy G_a = -V_prag_a - kappa * V_epist_a; lower G means a better action (Rule R8).
Actions are selected via softmax over negative expected free energy with inverse temperature beta_G (Rule R9).
The environment's ConsumeEffect reward of 1.0 is NOT used for decision-making; the agent's objective is internal expected free energy G.

Position coordinates, food source locations, food presence at current cell, grid dimensions, step count, and consumed_food flag are all extracted from perception but not direct inputs to the Kalman filter.
A spatial tie-breaking bonus (0.001 * distance improvement toward nearest food) is applied to movement actions' G values to aid navigation but is not part of the formal free-energy calculation.
B1 (Bayesian state tracking) can be tested: run 50 steps and assert abs(mu - h) < 0.15 on average, and that sigma2_q converges to a steady-state value.
B1 is testable: posterior variance sigma2_q should stabilise after a few steps of Kalman filtering.
B2 (Eating preference when depleted) can be tested: set mu=0.4 with food present and assert G['eat'] < G['stay'] < G['move_*'].
B2 is testable: when believed energy is well below setpoint, the eat action predicts the largest positive energy correction and thus lowest G.
B3 (Satiation reduces eating preference) can be tested: set mu=0.78 with food present and assert G['stay'] is comparable to or lower than G['eat'].
B3 is testable: near the setpoint, eating would overshoot and worsen V_prag, so stay becomes competitive or preferred.
B4 (Uncertainty drives exploration) can be tested: compare action distributions with kappa=0 vs kappa=0.5 and assert non-zero kappa produces more variable early action choices.
B4 is testable: the epistemic value term kappa*V_epist_a meaningfully alters action probabilities when uncertainty is high.
B5 (Navigation toward food) can be tested: place agent far from food with mu=0.5, run 20 steps, and assert agent position moves closer to nearest food.
B5 is testable but partly relies on the heuristic spatial tie-breaking rather than the formal free-energy objective.
B6 (Prediction error signals surprise) can be tested: inject a large noise sample and assert |epsilon_int| is large and |mu - mu_prior| is larger than normal.
B6 is testable: a large interoceptive prediction error propagates through Kalman gain to produce a larger posterior correction.
The formulation is grounded in Friston (2010) Free Energy Principle (Nature Reviews Neuroscience) and Petzschner et al. (2021) computational models of interoception (Trends in Neurosciences).
Keramati & Gutkin (2014) homeostatic reinforcement learning (eLife) is referenced for energy dynamics calibration.
The prior variance propagates as sigma2_q_prior = sigma2_q + sigma2_w each step before incorporating a new observation (Rule R3b).
The posterior variance updates as sigma2_q = (1.0 - K_gain) * sigma2_q_prior after incorporating the observation (Rule R4).
The true hidden energy h evolves as h = clip(h - lambda_decay + c_eat*ate - c_move*moved + N(0, sigma2_w), 0.0, 1.0) per step (Rule R1).
The formulation 'Drive-Reduction TD Q-Learning (Model-Free)' implements the Keramati & Gutkin (2014) model-free homeostatic reinforcement learning framework.
The Q-table is indexed by a joint state (grid_position, hunger_bin, action), combining external and internal state representations.
The nutritive value K=3.0 means consuming one food item reduces hunger by 3.0 units.

Primary reward is defined as drive reduction: r_t = D(h_t) - D(h_{t+1}), where D is a quadratic drive function.
Drive is computed as D(h_t) = m * |h_t - h*|^n with n=2 (quadratic), m=1.0, and h*=0.0.
Internal state dynamics follow: h_t_next = clip(h_t + lambda_drift - K * ate_t, 0, h_max).
The homeostatic reward prediction error (hRPE) is: delta_t = r_t + gamma * max_a' Q(s', h'_bin, a') - Q(s, h_bin, a).

Q-values are updated via: Q[(s_t, h_bin, a_t)] += alpha * delta_t, where the state is represented as a tuple incorporating the current state s_t, a history bin h_bin, and action a_t (i.e., Q(s, a) := Q(s, a) + alpha * delta_t with s encoded as (s_t, h_bin)).
The agent does NOT use the environment's reward signal from eat actions; it computes its own homeostatic reward internally.
Perception maps to external state s_t via (perception['x'], perception['y']).
Food presence at current position is determined by checking perception['resources']['food'] for any entry whose coordinates match (perception.x, perception.y).
Reward source is internally computed as homeostatic drive reduction; the environment ConsumeEffect reward=1.0 is explicitly not used.
Expected behavior B1 (testable): hunger increases monotonically by lambda_drift each step when no food is available until h_max.
B1 can be tested by running 50 steps with no food in the grid and asserting h_t increases each step by lambda_drift up to h_max.
Expected behavior B2 (testable): eating reduces drive and produces positive homeostatic reward.
B2 can be tested by setting h_t=5.0, executing eat at a food cell, and asserting h_t_next < h_t and r_t > 0.
Expected behavior B3 (testable): alliesthesia — eating near setpoint yields near-zero reward; eating at high deficit yields large positive reward.
B3 can be tested by comparing r_t when h_t=0.1 vs h_t=8.0 after eating.
Expected behavior B4 (testable): Q-values converge so that agents in high-hunger states preferentially select actions moving toward food.
B4 can be tested after 1000 training steps by verifying directional action bias toward nearest food under high-hunger states.
Expected behavior B5 (testable): agents do not seek food when sated (h_t ≈ 0), defaulting to stay or random movement.
B5 can be tested after training by setting h_t ≈ 0 and verifying the agent does not show directed food-seeking behavior.
The discount factor gamma=0.95 is strictly less than 1, satisfying the homeostatic optimality condition of postulate P3.
Hunger is discretized into h_bins=10 bins via: h_bin = min(int(h_t / h_max * h_bins), h_bins - 1).
The metabolic drift rate lambda_drift=0.1 increases hunger by 0.1 per timestep, creating ongoing homeostatic pressure.
Learning rate alpha has default value 0.1, a standard RL value for Q-value updates, following TD-learning conventions from Sutton & Barto (1998).

This formulation is a memoryless, reactive policy with no Q-table or learned value function.
The agent computes one-step-ahead utility for each action at every timestep.
Drive is computed as D(h_t) = m * |h_t - h*|^n (quadratic when n=2).
The drive function is sourced from Keramati & Gutkin (2014) postulate P1.
The homeostatic setpoint h* is fixed at 0.0 (no-deficit ideal state).
Hunger h_t increases by lambda_drift=0.1 each timestep due to metabolic drift.
Hunger h_t decreases by K=3.0 when food is consumed.
Hunger is bounded between 0.0 and h_max=10.0 via clipping.
Eating utility (U_eat) is the immediate drive reduction delta_D_eat, computed with one-step lookahead including metabolic drift.
Eating is gated by the satiation threshold eta=0.5: if D_t <= eta, U_eat is set to -1e9 (effectively impossible).
The satiation gate implements alliesthesia as described by Cabanac (1971).
Movement utility is U_move_a = w_prox * D_t * Prox_a, where Prox_a is the reduction in Manhattan distance to nearest food.
When D_t ≈ 0 (sated), movement utility ≈ 0 regardless of food proximity, implementing alliesthesia.
Stay utility U_stay = -(D_after_stay - D_t), which is always <= 0 as staying only costs drive.
The proximity weight w_prox=0.5 was tuned to balance approach behavior vs. immediate drive reduction.
After acting, only the internal hunger state h_t is updated (no Q-table update).
The environment reward from ConsumeEffect (reward=1.0) is NOT used; the agent relies on its own homeostatic utility computation.
Resource positions R are mapped from [(f['x'], f['y']) for f in perception['resources'].get('food', [])].

The formulation ID is 'free-energy-minimizing-homeostatic-agent-probabilistic-bayesian'.
The formulation is named 'Free-Energy Minimizing Homeostatic Agent (Probabilistic/Bayesian)'.
The formulation unifies homeostatic regulation with Bayesian inference and active exploration under a single free energy framework.
The formulation is based on Friston (2010) Free Energy Principle, Keramati & Gutkin (2014) P1 and P2, and Petzschner et al. (2021) on computational interoception.
The agent maintains a Gaussian posterior belief over hunger: mean mu_h and variance sigma_h_sq.
The agent maintains a grid-wide resource belief map R_beliefs where each cell holds P(food present).
The true hunger state h_t is not directly observed; the agent receives a noisy interoceptive observation o_h = h_t + N(0, sigma_obs).
The belief prediction step propagates hunger forward: mu_h_prior = mu_h + lambda_drift and sigma_h_sq_prior = sigma_h_sq + sigma_process_sq (Rule R1).
The Bayesian belief update uses Kalman-style Gaussian fusion: K_gain = sigma_h_sq_prior / (sigma_h_sq_prior + sigma_obs^2) (Rule R3).
The pragmatic value G_prag(a) is the negative expected squared deviation from setpoint divided by sigma_p^2 (Rule R4).
The epistemic value G_epist(a) is the binary entropy of the resource belief at the destination cell for movement actions (Rule R5).
G_epist is zero for 'eat' and 'stay' actions because no new cell is observed.
Total expected free energy is G(a) = G_prag(a) + w_e * G_epist(a) (Rule R6).
Action selection uses softmax over G with inverse temperature beta_G: P(a) ∝ exp(beta_G * G(a)) (Rule R7).
True hunger updates as h_t = clip(h_t + lambda_drift - K * ate_t, 0, h_max) each step (Rule R8).
Resource belief is set to 1.0 when food is confirmed at the current cell; it decays multiplicatively by (1 - kappa) when the cell is observed empty (Rule R9).
The environment reward from ConsumeEffect (reward=1.0) is NOT used; the agent is driven entirely by free energy minimization.
Food positions are derived from perception['resources']['food'] as a set of (x, y) tuples.

The ate_t flag (also referred to as ate_flag) is derived from the perception state: it is set to 1 when action == 'eat' and new_perception['last_action_result']['consumed'] is True (i.e., the eat action succeeded and a resource was consumed), else 0.
The hunger observation o_h is self-generated by the agent as h_t + N(0, sigma_obs), not provided by the environment.

The h_star default value is 0.0, representing zero hunger deficit as the homeostatic setpoint.
The sigma_p default value is 1.0, controlling the strength of homeostatic regulation via the width of the preferred-state prior.
The sigma_obs default value is 0.5, representing standard deviation of interoceptive noise.
The sigma_process_sq default value is 0.01, representing per-step uncertainty accumulation in the prediction phase.
The K default value is 3.0, representing hunger reduction per food item consumed.
The lambda_drift default value is 0.1, representing per-timestep metabolic hunger increase.
The h_max default value is 10.0, representing the simulation upper bound for hunger.
The beta_G default value is 5.0, controlling policy determinism in softmax action selection.
The w_e default value is 0.3, balancing epistemic (exploratory) versus pragmatic (exploitative) components of free energy.
The kappa default value is 0.05, controlling decay rate of resource beliefs for empty cells.
The initial_resource_prior default value is 0.05, approximating food_count / total_cells = 5/100.
The agent explores uncertain areas when hunger is low because the epistemic term dominates expected free energy.
The agent exploits known food sources when hunger is high because the pragmatic (drive-reducing) term dominates expected free energy.

The formulation id is 'continuous-free-energy-gradient-descent-with-precision-weighted-prediction-errors' and belongs to the active-inference paradigm.
The belief state μ(t) is updated each step via gradient descent on variational free energy F.
Variational free energy F is computed as the precision-weighted sum of squared sensory and prior prediction errors: F = 0.5 * pi_s * eps_s^2 + 0.5 * pi_p * eps_p^2.
The sensory prediction error eps_s = s_t - mu represents the mismatch between the interoceptive observation and the belief state.
The prior prediction error eps_p = mu - mu_p represents the mismatch between the belief state and the interoceptive setpoint.
The belief update rule is: mu = clip(mu + kappa * (pi_s * eps_s - pi_p * eps_p), 0, 1).
The agent uses a one-step lookahead to compute predicted free energy for each candidate action, then selects via softmax.
Action selection uses softmax over negative predicted free energy: logits = [-beta * F_pred[a] for a in available_actions].
The agent has no persistent Q-table or transition model; it relies purely on real-time prediction error minimization.
Internal energy increases by energy_gain_eat * food_palatability when a successful eat action is taken.
Internal energy decreases by c_move when any directional movement action is taken.
Internal energy decreases by c_stay during stay action or failed eat action.
Moving toward the nearest food source uses a reduced metabolic cost c_move_toward (0.03) compared to c_move (0.05), reflecting anticipatory regulation.
The interoceptive observation s_t is generated as: s_t = clip(energy + random_normal(0, sigma_s), 0, 1).
The interoceptive prior mu_p is fixed at 1.0, representing the fully sated energy target.
The prior precision pi_p is set to 2.0, higher than sensory precision pi_s (1.0), reflecting that priors over survival-compatible states are typically strong.
The sensory noise standard deviation sigma_s is set to 0.2, reflecting that interoceptive signals are inherently noisy.
The action inverse temperature beta is set to 5.0, providing a moderate exploitation bias.
The belief learning rate kappa is set to 0.5, following standard predictive coding conventions from Friston (2010).
The reward source for the environment is the eat action triggering a ConsumeEffect with env reward 1.0. Energy is tracked internally and is not taken from the environment reward directly.

The direction toward the nearest food is computed by finding the minimum Manhattan distance food resource and selecting the single-step action that reduces that distance.
The energy_gain_eat parameter is scaled by food palatability from the environment specification, so actual energy gain per eat event varies by food item.

Grid dimensions are read from perception['grid_width'] and perception['grid_height'] and used as a fallback distance when no food is present.

The formulation implements the Keramati & Gutkin (2014) framework establishing 'physiological rationality' via the mathematical duality between maximizing discounted drive-reduction reward and minimizing discounted homeostatic deviation.
The drive function D(h) = m * |h* - h|^n is formally equivalent to interoceptive surprise -ln p(h) under a Gaussian prior centered at h*.
State discretization yields 25 discrete states: 5 distance bins × 5 energy bins.
Distance bins are defined as: at food (0), near (1-2), medium (3-4), far (5-7), very far (8+).
The eat action is masked (logit set to -inf) when no food is present at the agent's current position.
The urgency inverse temperature beta_urgent=15.0 is strictly greater than the normal inverse temperature beta=8.0, ensuring more deterministic exploitation under critical energy.
gamma must be strictly less than 1 for the physiological rationality property to hold.
The agent's energy level h_t is normalized to [0, 1] and initialized at 0.5.
The homeostatic setpoint h_star is fixed throughout the simulation at 0.8.
The formulation implements a discrete Bayesian active inference agent that selects policies by minimizing expected free energy G(π).
Expected free energy G(π) decomposes into a pragmatic (goal-directed) term and an epistemic (information-seeking) term summed over the planning horizon T.
The interoceptive prior μ_p(t) is dynamic and shifts allostatically: it rises toward mu_p_base (0.8) in resource-rich environments and falls toward mu_p_low (0.5) in scarce environments.
The allostatic update is: mu_p ← mu_p + lambda_allo * (target - mu_p), where target = ρ * mu_p_base + (1 − ρ) * mu_p_low.
Resource richness ρ is computed as the number of visible food items divided by total visible cells.
The preference vector C is computed as C[o] = −eta * (energy_bin_center − mu_p)², making observations near the allostatic setpoint most preferred.
The agent maintains a learned transition model B[a] updated via Dirichlet counting with learning rate alpha_learn = 0.05.
Bayesian state inference predicts the future state by B[prev_action] @ q_s, then updates via element-wise multiplication with the observation likelihood row of A, normalized.
Policy enumeration generates K_policies = 18 candidate policies: 6 first-actions × 3 heuristic continuations over planning horizon T = 3.
Policy selection uses a softmax over −beta_G * G[π], with beta_G = 4.0 as the inverse temperature.
The likelihood matrix A is initialized as near-identity with 0.9 on the diagonal and 0.1/(|S|−1) off-diagonal.
The hidden state space is n_dist × n_energy = 4 × 5 = 20 discrete states.
The transition model Dirichlet counts b_counts are initialized to ones (uniform prior) and incremented by alpha_learn = 0.05 per observed transition.
B4 (transition model improvement) is testable: after 100 eating steps, B['eat'][high_energy_state, at_food_low_energy_state] should exceed 0.5.

B5 (preference vector shift) is testable: with mu_p=0.5, C for energy_bin=2 (center 0.5) should exceed C for energy_bin=4 (center 0.9).
B6 (multi-step planning superiority) is testable: agent with T=3 should accumulate more total food than agent with T=1 over 200 steps.
Food palatability is extracted per consumed food item with range [0.1, 1.0] from the environment spec.
Resource richness ρ is derived from len(perception['resources']['food']) divided by (grid_width * grid_height).
The environment reward of 1.0 from eating is not directly used by the agent; motivation comes from minimizing expected free energy G(π).
Eating increases energy by k_eat * food_palatability (clipped to [0,1]); movement decreases energy by c_move = 0.05; staying decreases energy by c_stay = 0.02.
The agent's internal energy is initialized at 0.5 and bounded to [0.0, 1.0].
The allostatic prior mu_p is initialized at 0.8 with range [0.3, 1.0].
The epistemic term in G(π) is the negative expected ambiguity: −Σ_s q_future[s] * H[A[:,s]], where H is column entropy of the likelihood matrix.
The pragmatic term in G(π) is the KL divergence between predicted observations q_obs and log-preferences C: Σ_o q_obs[o] * (log q_obs[o] − C[o]).

The decision loop each step is: update energy (R1) → update allostatic prior (R2) → recompute preferences (R3) → encode observation (R4) → Bayesian inference (R5) → enumerate policies (R6) → evaluate G(π) (R7) → select and execute action (R8); then in update(): learn transition model (R9).
This formulation is described as the most complete of three formulations, integrating planning, exploration, allostasis, and model learning.

References include Friston (2010) NRN on the free-energy principle, Petzschner et al. (2021) Trends in Neurosciences on interoception computational models, and Keramati & Gutkin (2014) eLife on homeostatic reinforcement learning.
The formulation id is 'hierarchical-precision-weighted-prediction-error-minimization-gradient-descent-ode' and belongs to the predictive-coding paradigm.
The formulation implements continuous-time ODE-based gradient descent on a precision-weighted variational free energy functional.
The generative model has two hierarchical levels: level-1 encodes expected immediate resource presence, level-2 encodes abstract environmental context (resource density).
Level-1 prediction error (eps_1) is computed as s - g_1(mu_1), where g_1 is an identity mapping (mu_1 directly predicts sensory vector s).
Level-2 prediction error (eps_2) is computed as mu_1 - g_2(mu_2), where g_2 maps scalar mu_2 to [mu_1[0], mu_1[1], mu_2, mu_2, 1.0 - mu_2].
Variational free energy F = 0.5 * Pi_1 * dot(eps_1, eps_1) + 0.5 * Pi_2 * dot(eps_2, eps_2) - 0.5 * (ln(Pi_1) + ln(Pi_2)).
Level-1 belief update is dmu_1_dt = kappa * (Pi_1 * eps_1 - Pi_2 * eps_2), integrated via Euler method with dt=1 and clipped to [0,1].
Level-2 belief update is dmu_2_dt = kappa * Pi_2 * dot([0,0,1,1,-1], eps_2), clipped to [0,1].
Precision at each level adapts via: Pi_l = Pi_l + eta_Pi * (1/(error_mag_sq_l + sigma2_init) - Pi_l), clipped to [0.01, 100.0].
Action selection uses softmax over negative predicted free energy: probs proportional to exp(beta * (-F_pred_a)).
If 'eat' is selected but no food is at the current position, its predicted free energy is set to 1e6 (strongly disfavored).
Perception maps to sensory vector s = [x/grid_width, y/grid_height, nearby_food_count/total_food, food_at_current_pos, avg_food_distance_normalized].
Average food distance is mean Manhattan distance to all food items, normalized by (grid_width + grid_height).
The inference loop runs N_iter=5 iterations of gradient-descent belief updates per decision step.
B1 (belief convergence) is testable: after 10 inference iterations with Pi_1 >> Pi_2, norm(mu_1 - s) should be < 0.1.
B2 (move toward food) is testable: with food at (5,5) and agent at (3,5), F_pred('move_right') should be less than F_pred('move_left').
B3 (eat when on food) is testable: when food is at agent's current position, 'eat' should be selected with high probability.
B4 (precision adaptation) is testable: over 50 steps in a stable environment Pi_1 should increase; after a sudden change Pi_1 should decrease.
B5 (hierarchical influence) is testable: setting mu_2 high (resource-rich context) should bias mu_1 food-presence components upward via top-down g_2 prediction.
Parameter kappa (inference_learning_rate) has default value 0.5, sourced from Rao & Ballard (1999).
Parameter Pi_1_init (initial_level1_precision) has default value 2.0, sourced from Friston (2010) indicating moderate sensory confidence.
Parameter Pi_2_init (initial_level2_precision) has default value 1.0, sourced from Friston (2010) indicating lower confidence for higher-level beliefs.
Parameter eta_Pi (precision_adaptation_rate) has default value 0.1, sourced from Friston (2010) as a slow precision learning rate.
Parameter N_iter (inference_iterations_per_step) has default value 5, balancing inference accuracy with computation cost.
Parameter beta (action_softmax_temperature) has default value 4.0, sourced from standard active inference implementations.

Successful eat action (reward > 0) triggers Pi_1 *= 1.1 (boosted sensory precision), confirming sensory model.
Failed eat action (reward == 0) triggers Pi_1 *= 0.9 (reduced sensory precision), penalizing incorrect prediction.
After action execution, the update() method re-encodes new perception and runs N_iter inference iterations to prepare for the next decision.
The Jacobian of g_1 with respect to mu_1 is the identity matrix, simplifying the level-1 gradient term.
The Jacobian of g_2 with respect to mu_2 is [0, 0, 1, 1, -1], used in the level-2 belief gradient computation.
Predicted free energy for a candidate action substitutes a predicted sensory vector s_pred (computed from the action's predicted outcome) into the level-1 prediction error term.
The formulation id is 'algebraic-precision-weighted-bayesian-filtering-single-step-conjugate-update' and it belongs to the predictive-coding paradigm.
The formulation implements a closed-form single-step Bayesian filtering agent using precision-weighted conjugate Gaussian inference with no iterative gradient descent.
At each tick, the agent combines its prior prediction with the sensory observation in one analytic step.
The prior prediction (mu_0) is computed by decaying the previous posterior (mu_hat) toward a zero-vector default baseline using temporal discount gamma=0.9 (Rule R1).
The prediction error (eps) is the signed difference between the sensory observation (s) and the prior prediction (mu_0) (Rule R2).
The posterior belief (mu_hat) is computed as the precision-weighted average: (pi_s * s + pi_0 * mu_0) / (pi_s + pi_0) (Rule R3).
The posterior precision is the sum of sensory and prior precisions (pi_s + pi_0), a property of conjugate Gaussian inference (Rule R4).
Precision-weighted surprise (w) is computed as pi_s * dot(eps, eps) / (pi_s + pi_0) (Rule R5).
Sensory precision (pi_s) is adapted toward 1 / (eps_sq + 0.01) at rate alpha_pi=0.15, clipped to [0.01, 100.0] (Rule R6).
Cell value for movement actions combines precision-weighted believed food value at destination with an uncertainty-penalized distance cost (Rule R7).
Action selection uses softmax over integrated (computed) action values with inverse temperature parameter beta=5.0 (Rule R8).
Initial sensory precision pi_s_init is 4.0, representing high initial trust in sensors (Friston 2010).
Initial prior precision pi_0_init is 1.0, representing moderate initial confidence in the internal model (Friston 2010).
The precision learning rate alpha_pi is 0.15, governing how quickly sensory precision adapts to prediction error variance.
Temporal discount gamma is 0.9, meaning the prior prediction decays 10% toward the default baseline each step (Friston & Kiebel 2009).
Softmax temperature beta is 5.0, making action selection moderately deterministic.
Penalty for eating when no food is present r_empty is -0.5, discouraging incorrect eat actions.
Maximum perception distance d_max is 5 Manhattan units; agent only encodes food within this distance.
The sensory vector s has 5 components: [food_here, nearest_food_dx_norm, nearest_food_dy_norm, food_density_nearby, avg_palatability].

Food sources are mapped from perception['resources'].get('food', []) as a list of {x, y, type, palatability} dicts. Using .get('food', []) ensures safe access when the 'food' key is absent, defaulting to an empty list.
Food palatability is extracted as f.get('palatability', 0.5) for each food item.
The last action result is read from perception.get('last_action_result', {}).

After a positive reward, sensory precision is increased by alpha_pi (reward confirms sensor accuracy).
After an unsuccessful eat action (reward=0), sensory precision is decreased by alpha_pi (wrong prediction reduces trust).
The uncertainty weight for movement penalization is computed as 1.0 - pi_s / (pi_s + pi_0), increasing when relative sensory confidence is lower.
The posterior mu_hat persists across steps as the basis for the next step's prior prediction via Rule R1.

Across all formulations (HRL, reactive drive dynamics, interoceptive active inference, predictive coding, and Bayesian free-energy), the drive function D(h) = m·|h − h*|^n with quadratic exponent n=2 and a shared metabolic decay rate λ≈0.02 per step serve as the universal quantitative backbone derived from Keramati & Gutkin (2014) and Hull (1943), with the key invariant that reward/utility is always defined as drive reduction r_t = D(h_t) − D(h_{t+1}), and the discount factor γ must be strictly less than 1 for homeostatic optimality — this parameter convergence across paradigms makes cross-formulation behavioral benchmarks (e.g., B1–B6 starvation/satiation tests) directly comparable.

The cluster reveals a systematic theory-to-implementation traceability pattern: every quantitative parameter (h*, n, γ, σ², κ, λ) is explicitly derived from one of five foundational postulates (P1–P5 spanning Cannon 1929, Hull 1943, Friston 2010, Keramati & Gutkin 2014, Conant & Ashby 1970), and each formulation maps its key variables to specific brain regions (hypothalamus→drive, VTA→RPE, insular cortex→interoceptive prediction error, nucleus accumbens→value), suggesting that future research runs should exploit this derivation graph to systematically test which postulate violations produce the most behaviorally distinguishable failures across agents.

The action space is structured into two functionally distinct categories — navigation actions (move_up, move_down, move_left, move_right, stay) and resource acquisition (eat) — where eat is conditionally available based on environmental perception (food presence), introducing context-dependent action masking that future models must account for.

This perception-gated availability of 'eat' creates a natural experimental contrast between unconditional actions (movement/stay) and conditional actions (eat), making it a useful testbed for studying how agents learn to modulate action selection based on sensory context versus pure policy optimization.

The co-authorship cluster linking Keramati and Gutkin (eLife.04811) alongside Friston (nrn2787) and Petzschner (tins.2020.09.012) suggests a convergent research network around computational and Bayesian frameworks of decision-making and interoception, with these papers likely spanning active inference, reinforcement learning, and predictive coding — indicating that future pipeline runs should explicitly map citation and conceptual bridges between these works to identify core theoretical lineages.

The presence of high-profile authors from distinct institutional backgrounds (e.g., Friston's free-energy/active inference program and Petzschner's Bayesian body perception work) sharing a thematic cluster implies that the pipeline is capturing a cross-disciplinary synthesis zone; future runs should flag such author-paper clusters for deeper semantic analysis to distinguish whether the connection is methodological (shared Bayesian formalism) or domain-specific (decision-making under uncertainty).

The hypothalamus-VTA-NAc axis implements a biologically grounded homeostatic temporal-difference learning circuit in which hypothalamic interoceptive drive signals gate the amplitude of phasic dopamine RPE signals at the VTA, and these modulated errors are then used by the Nucleus Accumbens to update action values and motivational salience — directly instantiating drive-reduction reward within a TD framework.

The modulation of dopaminergic RPE amplitude by current drive level (hunger, thirst, etc.) suggests that homeostatic state functions as a dynamic scaling parameter on the reward prediction error signal, providing a mechanistic target for research into how internal physiological need biases credit assignment and cue-driven motivation in reinforcement learning models of behavior.

Across pipeline runs, the state space discretization scheme is inconsistently parameterized: energy bins vary between n_energy_bins=5 and n_energy_bins=10, and distance bins vary between n_dist=4 and n_dist=5, yielding conflicting total state counts of 20, 25, or 50 — indicating that hyperparameter configurations are not being standardized or locked before experiments, which would confound any cross-run comparisons of Q-learning performance.

The Q-table is uniformly initialized to 0.0 across all (x, y, energy_bin) state-action pairs regardless of discretization resolution, suggesting that initialization strategy is held constant while bin granularity varies; future runs should systematically co-vary bin resolution and initialization to isolate the effect of state space granularity on convergence speed and policy quality.

Across all three facts, drive velocity (dD_dt) is consistently operationalized as a first-order finite difference (D_new − D_prev) rather than a continuous derivative, meaning the model approximates the instantaneous rate of drive change using only two temporally adjacent states — a design choice that prioritizes computational simplicity but may miss higher-frequency fluctuations in drive dynamics.

The drive velocity signal serves a directional diagnostic function — specifically distinguishing rising (worsening) from falling (improving) drive states — suggesting it is intended as a threshold-sensitive trigger for downstream regulatory or corrective mechanisms rather than a precise quantitative measure of change magnitude.

All four directional movement actions enforce grid boundary constraints exclusively through positional clipping (clamping to [0, grid_width-1] x [0, grid_height-1]), meaning boundary collision is handled implicitly rather than via explicit wall-detection logic — future research should test whether this clipping mechanism introduces gradient artifacts or boundary-adjacent behavioral biases in learned policies.

The pipeline has converged on a single, consistent mechanism for detecting successful eating: the binary `consumed` flag within `last_action_result` in the perception state, gated on the action being 'eat' — superseding the earlier heuristic that also used `reward > 0` as a fallback signal.

Future research runs should canonicalize the `ate_flag`/`just_ate`/`ate_t` variable naming and its derivation as `int(action == 'eat' and perception['last_action_result'].get('consumed', False))` to eliminate redundant contradictions and memory updates around this feature.

The canonical pattern for accessing food resources from agent perception is `perception['resources'].get('food', [])`, which safely handles absent keys and returns a list of dicts with fields {x, y, type, palatability}; downstream coordinate extraction consistently maps these to (x, y) tuples or sets for spatial comparison against the agent's current position.

Food presence detection at the agent's current position requires matching entries in the food list against (perception['x'], perception['y']), indicating that future research runs should standardize a reusable helper function for this positional lookup to avoid redundant and potentially inconsistent coordinate-matching logic across agents.

All five behavioral assertions (B1–B5) are empirically testable through simulation-based methods, each with a distinct measurement modality: controlled initial conditions (B1), visit count comparison (B2), action probability monitoring (B3), time-series state monitoring (B4), and logged prediction error statistics (B5) — suggesting a comprehensive, modality-diverse test suite can be constructed entirely within a simulated environment.

B4 in particular requires simulation as a prerequisite and relies on statistical assertions over a time series of a scalar variable h, indicating that longitudinal state monitoring — rather than single-point measurement — is the appropriate experimental design for validating temporal or convergence-related behavioral properties.

Bayesian belief convergence (B1) is consistently operationalizable via precision-weighted posterior tracking: when sensory precision dominates prior precision (Pi_s >> Pi_0 or Pi_1 >> Pi_2), the posterior mean mu_hat reliably converges within a tight norm threshold (<0.1–0.15) of the sensory observation, providing a concrete and reproducible quantitative test criterion across multiple formulations.

Kalman filter steady-state behavior and calibrated belief tracking (B1/B3) are jointly testable through two complementary convergence criteria: posterior variance sigma2_q should stabilize within a few filtering steps, and the tracking error abs(mu_h - h_t) should remain bounded within 2*sigma_obs on average over 100+ steps — together these criteria validate both the precision and accuracy of dynamic belief updating in the pipeline.

The agent's state representation underwent a simplification: an earlier richer encoding—(x, y, food_at_cell, nearest_food_dx_sign, nearest_food_dy_sign, hunger_bin)—was contradicted and replaced by a coarser joint state (grid_position, hunger_bin, action), suggesting a deliberate trade-off between representational expressiveness and tabular Q-learning tractability (state-space size reduction).

The consistent use of tabular Q-learning over a joint external-internal state space (grid position × discretized hunger) indicates that hunger is treated as a first-class state variable rather than a reward shaping signal, which has direct implications for how motivational drives are architecturally encoded in future agent designs.

The agent employs a Dirichlet-based online transition model (B[a]) initialized with a uniform prior (counts = 1) and updated incrementally at a conservative learning rate (α = 0.05 per observed transition), reflecting a design choice that heavily weights prior beliefs over new evidence and slows model adaptation — a deliberate regularization strategy worth examining against environments with faster dynamics.

The consistent use of α_learn = 0.05 across all action-conditioned Dirichlet count tables indicates a global, action-invariant learning rate, which may under-fit transitions for frequently visited action-state pairs while over-fitting sparse ones; future research should investigate action- or state-conditioned adaptive learning rates to balance plasticity and stability across the transition model.

The model implements a two-stage Bayesian filtering loop: a prediction step where the prior is propagated via action-conditioned transition matrices (s_prior = B[action] @ s_belief) and an update step where the posterior is computed by element-wise multiplication of the prior with the observation likelihood column of A, normalized — with a uniform fallback when the normalization constant approaches zero, ensuring numerical stability.

The strict separation between the transition model B (encoding action-conditioned state dynamics) and the likelihood model A (encoding observation-state mappings) mirrors the standard Hidden Markov Model/Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) belief update structure, suggesting future research runs should treat B and A as independently learnable or intervenable components to isolate the contributions of action-conditioned dynamics versus perceptual uncertainty on inference accuracy.

The two-level hierarchical generative model implements a principled separation of timescales: level-1 encodes a 5-dimensional immediate environmental state (e.g., local resource presence), while level-2 compresses abstract environmental context into a scalar resource density estimate (mu_2), enabling efficient top-down predictions that gate resource-relevant dimensions without interfering with positional dimensions.

The asymmetric top-down prediction architecture — where position dimensions pass through unmodified but resource dimensions are predicted from the level-2 scalar — suggests that the model assigns causal structure to resource availability (context-dependent) versus spatial location (context-independent), a design choice that could be empirically tested by ablating the selective gating to assess its contribution to belief updating and foraging efficiency.

Reward-contingent precision modulation follows a symmetric Hebbian-like update rule: sensory precision (Pi_1) is multiplicatively scaled by a fixed factor alpha_pi (e.g., ×1.1 on success, ×0.9 on failure), directly coupling prediction accuracy at the action level to the reliability weight assigned to sensory input in the generative model.

This bidirectional precision update mechanism operationalizes a closed-loop credibility signal — successful eat actions reinforce sensory trust while failed actions attenuate it — suggesting future research should test whether asymmetric alpha_pi values (different boost vs. penalty rates) produce more stable or faster-converging belief dynamics under sparse reward schedules.
